title: Invariant Gibbs measures for the cubic nonlinear wave equation
speaker: Bjoern Bringmann
abstract: In this talk, we prove the invariance of the Gibbs measure for the three-dimensional cubic nonlinear wave equation, which is also known as the hyperbolic Φ43-model.   In the first half of this talk, we illustrate our main objects and questions through Hamiltonian ODEs, which serve as a toy-model. We also connect our theorem with classical and recent developments in constructive QFT, dispersive PDEs, and stochastic PDEs.

proof_stages.label: Finite-dimensional invariant-measure template
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a coercive quartic potential, the speaker explains the associated Hamiltonian ODE, Gibbs measure, Liouville/energy proof of invariance, and analogous Langevin dynamics as the finite-dimensional model for the rest of the talk.

proof_stages.label: Infinite-dimensional phi-four problem and historical gap
proof_stages.summary: The ODE template is transferred formally to fields on the torus, producing the phi^4_d Gibbs measure and heat, wave, and Schrodinger dynamics, with the central questions of measure construction, probabilistic well-posedness, and invariance placed in the known historical table.

proof_stages.label: Three-dimensional renormalized cubic wave theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated: the renormalized cubic nonlinear wave equation on T^3 is probabilistically globally well posed for Gibbs-distributed data and preserves the phi^4_3 Gibbs measure, despite singularity with respect to the Gaussian free field and regularity about -1/2-.

proof_stages.label: Renormalized construction of the phi-four-three measure
proof_stages.summary: The formal infinite-dimensional Gibbs density is made rigorous by finite-dimensional or truncated approximations, absorption of quadratic divergences, quartic counterterms with divergent constants, and convergence in a negative Sobolev-type space to the phi^4_3 measure.

proof_stages.label: Caloric reparametrization of Gibbs samples
proof_stages.summary: A Gibbs sample is evolved by the invariant stochastic cubic heat equation and replaced by its time-one value, yielding caloric initial data decomposed into a rough Gaussian component, a smoother cubic Gaussian component, and a still smoother remainder.

proof_stages.label: Paracontrolled local wave ansatz
proof_stages.summary: The local wave solution is expanded into seven terms: explicit stochastic wave objects, paracontrolled components absorbing dangerous high-low and high-high-low interactions, and a residual y, organized around the subcritical regularity gap near 1/2.

proof_stages.label: Heat-wave estimates and cancellation mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: The proof of the local theory relies on estimates for mixed heat/wave propagator objects and on algebraic groupings, especially the 1533 cancellation, that turn individually divergent products into convergent combinations.

proof_stages.label: Globalization and invariant dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The local probabilistic solution theory is then globalized by the Bourgain invariant-measure paradigm, completing the claimed global well-posedness and invariance result, though this final step is only briefly mentioned.

examples.name: lambda=0 harmonic oscillator/Gaussian Gibbs case
examples.note: The linear oscillator case shows that when the quartic coupling is absent the finite-dimensional Gibbs measure reduces to a Gaussian baseline, making the later non-Gaussian and singular PDE behavior easier to contrast.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the oscillator setup to lambda=0, notes the quartic Gibbs factor becomes constant, and identifies the resulting Gibbs measure as Gaussian for the linear harmonic oscillator

passes.reason: The zero-coupling case removes the quartic interaction, making the boundary/free Gaussian harmonic oscillator, used only as a baseline illustration.
The λ=0 free case is the boundary coupling where the quartic interaction vanishes, leaving the rotationally symmetric Gaussian harmonic oscillator.
The lambda=0 case is the free linear/Gaussian boundary case of the quartic family with maximal quadratic regularity, used as a base comparison rather than to refute anything.
λ=0 is the boundary free quadratic oscillator, removing the quartic interaction and giving the highly symmetric Gaussian phase-space case used as a baseline.
the lambda=0 case is the free Gaussian boundary case of the interacting quartic family, with the quartic interaction absent

examples.name: Finite-dimensional Langevin diffusion in a quartic potential
examples.note: The Brownian-driven Langevin SDE is used as a finite-dimensional stochastic analogue of the heat dynamics and to illustrate why noise is added to compensate gradient-flow dissipation.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the finite-dimensional Langevin SDE, its invariant density e^{-V}, relates it to the Gibbs measure, and explains the role of stochastic forcing for the quartic potential setting

examples.name: Odd cubic potential losing coercivity
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker points out that a q^3-type potential has no lower bound, illustrating why the talk restricts to even defocusing powers.
examples.engagement_reason: the odd cubic potential is only used to answer a question by stating that q^3 lacks a lower bound and hence loses coercivity

examples.name: Adding a first time derivative to the wave equation
examples.note: In the final questions, a damping or first-time-derivative variant is used to illustrate that dissipation changes the invariant-measure story unless paired with stochastic forcing.
examples.engagement_reason: adding a first time derivative is discussed only qualitatively as damping or as leading to heat-type variants, with no concrete equation analysis or derivation

passes.reason: Adding a first time derivative gives a damped variant and is noted to destroy invariance unless compensating stochastic forcing is added.
